userPrompt: Cluster A — Define Solve. Master plan 20260430-02-rebuild-design-derivation, sub-sprint cluster-a-define-solve. Define a new resolution-claim element category for the design-large-task proof MCP — what observable state counts as the problem being solved. Today the proof has five element types (Necessary Condition, Evidence, Rule, Permission, Risk); the closure check verifies element well-formedness but does NOT validate against the problem statement; brief Acceptance Criteria are agent-imagined at render time with no upstream source. Cluster A settles: (1) naming for the new category (Sufficient Condition / completion criterion / resolution criterion / exit condition / other), (2) shape (new sixth element type vs NC extension vs alternative), (3) schema (required fields, source rule, integrity-check semantics, basis-link semantics), (4) closure-check extension (presence + coverage + non-restrictiveness verification), (5) hypothesis-methodology criteria (what makes a problem statement well-formed enough to carry resolution claims). Five attributes the new element must carry are designer-locked: Observable, Designer-ratified, Problem-statement-anchored, Forward-looking, Non-restrictive. RULE-1..10 from master plan inherited as Rules. Five existing element types are immutable. Phase 4b is presumed valid; cluster A's decisions may force closure-check or schema extensions handled inside cluster A. design-specify generates solutions; the proof produces specify's input.

problem_articulation.text: The proof has no element category that says what observable state counts as the problem being solved. Acceptance criteria today are agent-imagined prose at brief-render time, with no upstream definition source.
problem_articulation.current_behavior: Brief authors acceptance criteria as free prose at Closure with no MCP tracking and no link back to necessary conditions or the problem statement.
problem_articulation.desired_behavior: A structurally-typed element category carries resolution claims that are observable, designer-ratified, anchored to the problem statement, forward-looking, and non-restrictive.

problem_articulation.text: The closure check gate verifies six structural properties of elements but does not reference the problem statement. The hypothesis-to-resolution link is structurally absent.
problem_articulation.current_behavior: Closure verifies necessary-condition grounding, collapse tests, rejected alternatives, post-transition revision, minimum rounds, and existence — none against the problem statement.
problem_articulation.desired_behavior: Closure verifies that resolution claims collectively cover the problem statement's named aspects and that each claim is anchored to a specific aspect.

success_criteria.text: The new element category's name is locked.
success_criteria.observable: A single canonical name appears in the cluster A design brief and is referenced consistently across the proof, brief template, and downstream skills.

success_criteria.text: The closure-check extension is drafted in the brief.
success_criteria.observable: The brief specifies presence, coverage, and non-restrictiveness verification semantics for the new element category.

success_criteria.text: Hypothesis-methodology criteria are defined or explicitly deferred with rationale.
success_criteria.observable: Either the brief carries problem-statement well-formedness criteria, or it states the deferral and the reason.

success_criteria.text: Cluster A's brief, spec, plan, and execute-write all complete; tests pass on main.
success_criteria.observable: The cluster A sub-sprint merges with green tests and an archive commit on main.

done_state_vision.text: The brief renders a Resolution Claims section drawn from structurally-typed proof elements, each entry traceable to a named aspect of the problem statement, each designer-ratified.
done_state_vision.imagined_moment: Reading a design brief from a future sprint and seeing the Resolution Claims section list four claims, each tagged with a stable identifier, each linking back to a named aspect of the problem statement section, each carrying a one-line observable test — and knowing none of them came from agent imagination at render time.

done_state_vision.text: design-specify reads the brief and produces spec acceptance criteria mechanically from the resolution-claim entries — no guessing, no agent-authored material at the brief-to-spec boundary.
done_state_vision.imagined_moment: Watching design-specify run and seeing the spec's acceptance criteria emerge as direct derivatives of brief resolution-claim entries, with the spec-side numbering scheme overlaid mechanically.

constraint_envelope.text: The five existing element categories are immutable. Their schemas, source rules, and required fields cannot change in this sprint.
Whatever shape is chosen for the resolution-claim element must carry all five locked attributes — observable, designer-ratified, problem-statement-anchored, forward-looking, non-restrictive.
Cluster A executes first under the master plan; cluster B reads cluster A's output as locked rules; cluster C reads both as locked rules. No parallel execution.

design-specify is the solution generator. The proof produces input for specify; specify owns architectural shape choice. Resolution claims are input constraints for specify, not architectural decisions.
The phase 4a output language must transition into phase 4b without translation. Whatever structural shape resolution claims take must be producible by phase 4a (cluster C scope).

Phase 4b is presumed valid and sufficient; the default for cluster B is confirmation, not refactor. Cluster A's decisions may force closure-check extensions and proof-MCP source updates handled inside cluster A.

scope_boundary.text: Modifying any of the five existing element categories is out of scope. Their schemas are locked.
The phase 4a-to-phase 4b transition mechanism is out of scope. Cluster B owns it.
Restructuring phase 4a understanding flow is out of scope. Cluster C owns it.
Architectural choice for downstream consumers is out of scope. design-specify owns it.
Brief-template revisions to render the resolution-claim section are borderline — minor wording changes are in scope; restructuring the eight-section brief shape is not.
scope_boundary.source: cluster A scope inferred from brief-template touch surface

personal_use_case_map.text: Designer running cluster A under the master plan now, holding master-plan context fresh.
personal_use_case_map.scenario: This sprint's design conversation; settling cluster A's open decisions in the next several rounds.

personal_use_case_map.text: Designer or another reader returning to the cluster A brief during clusters B and C, when the locked decisions become inherited rules.
personal_use_case_map.scenario: Reading cluster A's brief while running cluster B's design conversation; quoting the resolution-claim shape into cluster B's transition design.
personal_use_case_map.context_assumed_lost: The naming-debate alternatives, the shape-decision rationale, and which option-vs-option arguments were considered and discarded — only the locked decision itself will remain salient unless the brief captures the rejected alternatives explicitly.

personal_use_case_map.text: design-specify agent reading the cluster A brief later, dispatching architects and writing the spec.
personal_use_case_map.scenario: design-specify consumes the brief envelope; resolution-claim entries seed the spec's acceptance criteria.
personal_use_case_map.context_assumed_lost: Everything except the brief itself — design-specify has no design-conversation context. Whatever resolution-claim shape we land on must be readable from the brief alone.

cost_energy_budget.text: Cluster A is foundation work — every downstream cluster reads its decisions as locked rules. Effort tolerance is high for getting the shape right; low for repeated revisits later.
Implementation envelope for adding a sixth element category is small — eight files, each touched in a small mechanical way. Complexity ceiling is well within budget.

project_fit.text: Adding a new typed element to the proof grammar fits the existing project convention — the proof's element-type registration pattern is consistent and well-trodden, used by the decision-record sprint as a precedent for vocabulary reuse.
project_fit.project_convention_referenced: proof grammar as stable, reusable vocabulary across consumers

project_fit.text: Closing the agent-imagined acceptance-criteria gap is aligned with the project's broader pattern of redirecting agent-completion-drive into structured channels rather than prohibiting it.

open_questions_ledger.text: What is the canonical name for the new element category? Working name 'resolution claim' versus field candidates (sufficient condition, completion criterion, validation criterion, exit condition, post-condition, achievement goal). Naming will propagate downstream.
What structural shape — new sixth element category, extension of the necessary-condition shape, or alternative? The five locked attributes constrain the choice but don't force one.

How does the closure check verify presence, coverage, and especially non-restrictiveness for the new element category? Non-restrictiveness is structurally hard to check from the outside; this question may force the shape decision.

Do we need formal criteria for problem-statement well-formedness, and if so what are they? Today the problem statement is a free string; coverage tracing cannot be mechanical without some structural shape.

glossary.definition: Working name for the new proof element category cluster A is defining. The structurally-typed claim that says what observable, designer-ratified, forward-looking, problem-statement-anchored, non-restrictive state counts as the problem being solved. Today this concept exists informally as agent-prose Acceptance Criteria at brief-render time with no upstream source.

source.citation: master-plan §2 Endstate Point 5; industry-explorer 2026-04-30 naming-candidates table

sense_constraints: NOT Acceptance Criteria (deprecated agent-prose form)
NOT Rule (Rules restrict the design space; resolution claims certify exit)

source.citation: master-plan §2 Endstate Point 5; industry-explorer 2026-04-30 naming-candidates table

history.reason: Designer locked canonical name. 'Resolve Condition' aligns structurally with 'Necessary Condition' — both end in 'Condition', pluralize naturally, give the proof grammar a parallel pairing without the misleading necessary/sufficient logical connotation.

glossary.definition: Existing proof element type. Something that must be true for the design to hold. A constraint any candidate solution must satisfy. Required fields: grounding (≥1 EVIDENCE/RULE/PERMISSION), reasoning_chain (IF premises THEN necessary), collapse_test (what breaks if removed), optional rejected_alternatives. Designer-locked as immutable per master-plan RULE-1.
sense_constraints: Schema is immutable for this sprint
An NC restricts; a resolution claim does not

glossary.definition: Existing proof element type. Codebase, industry, or practitioner-friction facts the design rests on. Verifiable. Source must NOT be designer (agent-sourced only). Designer-locked as immutable per master-plan RULE-1.
sense_constraints: Source rule prohibits designer-sourced evidence

glossary.definition: Existing proof element type. Designer-directed restriction on the design space. Source must be designer. Restricts the envelope of valid approaches. Designer-locked as immutable per master-plan RULE-1.
sense_constraints: Designer-sourced only
Restricts; a resolution claim certifies exit without restricting

glossary.definition: Existing proof element type. Designer-directed relief from a previously-stated Rule. Names what restriction is relaxed via a 'relieves' field. Designer-locked as immutable per master-plan RULE-1.
sense_constraints: Designer-sourced only

glossary.definition: Existing proof element type. Identified hazard attached to a specific element via a 'basis' field. A failure mode that could materialize if a premise is wrong. Designer-locked as immutable per master-plan RULE-1.

source.citation: proof.js (no type-specific block); metrics.js completeness counter
proof.js (no type-specific block); metrics.js completeness counter

glossary.definition: The designer's confirmed framing of what is being solved. Initialized at Solve stage opening, stored in proof state. Today is a free string with no structural shape requirement; cluster A may need to formalize what 'named aspects' means for coverage tracing.
sense_constraints: NOT a list of constraints
NOT a solution sketch — describes pain, not approach

glossary.definition: Understand stage of the Interview Loop. Builds shared problem-side understanding via discovery flow. Output transitions to Phase 4b. Name designer-locked per master-plan RULE-2.
Solve stage of the Interview Loop. Receives the pre-seeded proof from Phase 4a and continues building under the Design Proof MCP. Name designer-locked per master-plan RULE-2.

The proof MCP's gate function (metrics.js:202-274) verifying six structural properties before allowing closure: NCs grounded, NCs have collapse tests, ≥1 NC has rejected alternatives, ≥1 element revised after phase transition, minimum 3 rounds, ≥1 active NC. Today does NOT reference the problem statement — this is the structural gap cluster A is targeting.

What the design cannot violate, must respect, or must avoid. Today fully expressible in the five immutable element types (NC, Rule, Permission, Evidence, Risk). One of three artifacts design-specify needs from the proof.

Trace structure ensuring every aspect of the problem statement maps to at least one constraint and at least one resolution criterion. Concrete shape settled inside cluster A or cluster B. One of three artifacts design-specify needs from the proof.

The criteria that make a problem statement well-formed enough to carry resolution claims at Phase 4b opening. Today no such criteria exist (problem statement is a free string). Cluster A may need to formalize 'named aspects' so coverage tracing is mechanical rather than semantic.

glossary.definition: Designer validates an agent-proposed formal proof. The agent authors the proposal; the designer's confirmation is the validation gate. Distinct from designer-sourced (where the designer originates the content, as with Rules and Permissions). One of the five locked attributes a resolution claim must carry.
sense_constraints: NOT designer-sourced — agent authors, designer validates
Validation, not authorship

history.designer_quote: For designer ratified definition, it is designer will validate an agent proposed formal proof
history.reason: Designer clarified meaning of one of the five locked resolution-claim attributes. Distinct from designer-sourced.

glossary.definition: A PM-named dimension of the problem that the proof targets for resolution. Extracted from the problem statement at Solve Stage opening, agent-proposed and PM-ratified, carries a stable ID. Resolve Conditions anchor to a Concern via problem_anchor; the coverage check at closure verifies every Concern has at least one ratified Resolve Condition or anchored Rule covering it.
sense_constraints: NOT a solution sketch — names the problem dimension, not what fixes it
NOT a constraint — that is a Rule's job; a Concern names a dimension where outcome matters
NOT a Resolve Condition — a Concern is the target; the Resolve Condition is the claim about how that target resolves
NOT free prose — a Concern is a stable ID with a short label

history.designer_quote: lets go with 'concern' because in my my mind that matches problem well...here is our problem with these concerns
history.reason: Designer locked the coverage-anchor name. 'Concern' matches PM speech ('here is our problem with these concerns'). Also: drop 'pain' / 'pain-point' language across the proof.
